pattern: Once a string is chopped into equal chunks, rearranging those chunks freely means only the MULTISET of chunks matters, not their order -- so the whole question collapses into a multiset-equality check.
hints: Cutting a string into k equal-length pieces and letting them be freely reordered means the final result can be any concatenation of those pieces in any order.
Two strings built from the same pool of pieces in some order are reachable from each other exactly when they're built from the SAME pieces, counted with the same multiplicity.
Slice both strings into their k equal chunks and compare the two chunk collections as multisets, ignoring order entirely.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, two chunks swap places
statement example, three chunks reorder to match
statement example, chunking too coarse to match
k equals string length, single-character chunks
k equals 1, whole string must already match
same letters overall but chunk boundaries split a needed pair

stress.description: random anagram pairs built by shuffling equal chunks
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    k = rng.randint(1, 20)
    chunk_len = rng.randint(1, 30)
    n = k * chunk_len
    import string
    chunks = []
    for _ in range(k):
        chunks.append(''.join(rng.choice(string.ascii_lowercase) for _ in range(chunk_len)))
    s = ''.join(chunks)
    shuffled = chunks[:]
    rng.shuffle(shuffled)
    t = ''.join(shuffled)
    return [s, t, k]

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def isPossibleToRearrange(self, s, t, k):
        n = len(s)
        L = n // k
        s_chunks = sorted(s[i:i + L] for i in range(0, n, L))
        t_chunks = sorted(t[i:i + L] for i in range(0, n, L))
        return s_chunks == t_chunks
